questions.stem: Which process results in a chemical change?

parts.prompt: Which process results in a chemical change?

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: Heat liquid water to form steam.

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: Mix powdered carbon and iron filings.

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: Heat sulfur in air to make sulfur dioxide.

common_mistakes.feedback: A is a physical change. Water boiling to steam only changes state - the substance is still H2O, so no new substance is formed.
B is just mixing. The carbon is still carbon and the iron is still iron - you could even separate them with a magnet. No new substance is formed, so it is not a chemical change.
C is a physical change. Melting only changes iron from solid to liquid; it is still iron. A chemical change would produce a new substance.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: D
A chemical change is one in which a new substance is formed. When sulfur is heated in air it reacts with oxygen to make sulfur dioxide (SO2), a brand-new compound. This is combustion, which is a chemical reaction and is exothermic.

The other options are physical changes:
• A boiling water → still H2O, just a state change.
• B mixing carbon and iron filings → no reaction; they can be separated (e.g. using a magnet).
• C melting iron → still iron, just solid to liquid.
chapter_refs: C6.01 Physical and chemical changes
Physical change: a change in the physical state, form or appearance of a substance that does not produce any new chemical substance.
Chemical change (chemical reaction): a change in which one or more new substances are formed; usually difficult to reverse and accompanied by an energy change.
Combustion: a chemical reaction in which a substance reacts with oxygen and releases energy; combustion is exothermic.

questions.stem: Which statement indicates that a chemical change occurs?

parts.prompt: Which statement indicates that a chemical change occurs?

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: There is a change in colour.

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: There is a change of state.

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: There is no change in mass.

common_mistakes.feedback: Energy being absorbed can happen in physical changes too (e.g. ice melting absorbs heat), so absorbing energy alone does not prove a chemical change has occurred.
A change of state (melting, freezing, boiling, condensing) is the classic example of a PHYSICAL change — no new substance is formed, just a different state of the same substance.
Mass is always conserved in a closed system, whether the change is physical or chemical, so 'no change in mass' tells you nothing about which type of change happened.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: B — There is a change in colour.
A chemical change (chemical reaction) is one in which one or more new substances are formed. A colour change is one of the classic clues that a new substance has been produced.

Why the others are wrong:
• A (energy absorbed) — energy can also be absorbed in a physical change such as melting ice, so this is not a reliable clue.
• C (change of state) — melting, freezing, boiling and condensing are physical changes (same substance, different state).
• D (no change in mass) — mass is conserved in every closed-system change, physical or chemical.
chapter_refs: C6.01 Physical and chemical changes
Physical change: a change in the physical state, form or appearance of a substance that does not produce any new chemical substance.
Chemical change (chemical reaction): a change in which one or more new substances are formed; usually difficult to reverse and accompanied by an energy change.

questions.stem: Which process involves a chemical change?

parts.prompt: Which process involves a chemical change?

common_mistakes.feedback: Condensation is a physical change. A gas cools into a liquid (e.g. steam to water), but it is still the same substance - no new substance is formed.
Evaporation is a physical change. A liquid becomes a gas (e.g. water to water vapour). The particles are the same, so there is no new substance.
Melting is a physical change. A solid becomes a liquid (e.g. ice to water); still the same substance, just in a different state.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: D
A chemical change produces a new substance. In oxidation, a substance gains oxygen (or loses electrons) and a new compound is formed - for example magnesium + oxygen → magnesium oxide, or iron rusting to form iron oxide. This is a chemical reaction.

The other options are changes of state, which are physical changes:
• A condensation: gas → liquid
• B evaporation: liquid → gas
• C melting: solid → liquid
In each case the substance is the same before and after.
chapter_refs: C6.01 Physical and chemical changes
C6.03 Redox reactions
Physical change: a change in the physical state, form or appearance of a substance that does not produce any new chemical substance.
Chemical change (chemical reaction): a change in which one or more new substances are formed; usually difficult to reverse and accompanied by an energy change.
Oxidation (oxygen view): the gain of oxygen by an element or compound during a chemical reaction.

questions.stem: Which process is a physical change?

parts.prompt: Which process is a physical change?

common_mistakes.feedback: Electrolysis uses electricity to split a compound into new substances (for example copper chloride splits into copper and chlorine). Because new substances are formed, it is a chemical change, not a physical change.
Oxidation means a substance gains oxygen (or loses electrons), like magnesium burning to form magnesium oxide. A brand new substance is made, so oxidation is a chemical change.
Polymerisation joins small monomer molecules into a long polymer chain, which is a different substance from the monomers. So polymerisation is a chemical change, not a physical change.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: B (melting)
A physical change only alters the state, form or appearance of a substance and no new substance is formed.
When ice melts to water the substance is still H2O - only the physical state has changed, and freezing will reverse it.
Electrolysis, oxidation and polymerisation all make new substances, so they are chemical changes.
chapter_refs: C6.01 Physical and chemical changes
Physical change: a change in the physical state, form or appearance of a substance that does not produce any new chemical substance.
Chemical change (chemical reaction): a change in which one or more new substances are formed; usually difficult to reverse and accompanied by an energy change.

questions.stem: Which process is a physical change? A addition of carbon dioxide to limewater; B addition of sodium to water; C electrolysis of molten lead(II) bromide; D separation of petroleum by fractional distillation.

parts.prompt: Which process is a physical change?

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: addition of carbon dioxide to limewater

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: separation of petroleum by fractional distillation

common_mistakes.feedback: Adding CO2 to limewater forms a white solid (calcium carbonate). A precipitate appearing is a classic sign that a NEW substance has formed, so this is a chemical change, not a physical one.

Sodium reacts vigorously with water, fizzing and producing hydrogen gas and sodium hydroxide. A gas being produced and a new solution forming are both signs that new substances are made — so this is a chemical change.
Electrolysis of molten PbBr2 splits the compound into lead metal and bromine gas. Two new substances are formed, so this is a chemical change (a decomposition).

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: D — separation of petroleum by fractional distillation.
A physical change only alters the state, form or situation of a substance and does NOT produce a new substance. Fractional distillation boils and condenses the different hydrocarbons in petroleum at their different boiling points — the molecules are just separated, not turned into new molecules.

Why the others are chemical changes (a new substance is formed each time):
• A — CO2 + limewater forms calcium carbonate (the milky white precipitate).
• B — sodium + water forms sodium hydroxide + hydrogen gas.
• C — electrolysis decomposes PbBr2 into lead metal and bromine gas.
chapter_refs: C6.01 Physical and chemical changes
Physical change: a change in the physical state, form or appearance of a substance that does not produce any new chemical substance.
Chemical change (chemical reaction): a change in which one or more new substances are formed; usually difficult to reverse and accompanied by an energy change.

questions.stem: Which processes involve a chemical change? 1 cracking of an alkane. 2 polymerisation of ethene. 3 corrosion of iron.

parts.prompt: Which processes involve a chemical change? 1 cracking of an alkane. 2 polymerisation of ethene. 3 corrosion of iron.

common_mistakes.feedback: You missed corrosion. When iron corrodes it reacts with oxygen to form iron oxide (rust) - that is a new substance, so corrosion is a chemical change too.
You missed polymerisation. Polymerisation joins many ethene molecules into a long poly(ethene) chain - a brand-new substance is formed, so it is a chemical change.
You missed cracking. Cracking breaks a large alkane molecule into smaller molecules (a smaller alkane and an alkene) - new substances are formed, so cracking is a chemical change.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: A (1, 2 and 3)
The test for a chemical change is simple: is a new substance formed?

• 1 Cracking of an alkane - a long alkane molecule splits into a smaller alkane and an alkene. Different molecules are formed → chemical change.
• 2 Polymerisation of ethene - many ethene molecules (C2H4) join to make the polymer poly(ethene), a completely different substance → chemical change.
• 3 Corrosion of iron - iron reacts with oxygen (and water) to form iron oxide (rust). Iron gains oxygen, which is oxidation → chemical change.

All three processes form new substances, so the answer is A.
chapter_refs: C6.01 Physical and chemical changes
C6.03 Redox reactions
Chemical change (chemical reaction): a change in which one or more new substances are formed; usually difficult to reverse and accompanied by an energy change.
Oxidation (oxygen view): the gain of oxygen by an element or compound during a chemical reaction.

questions.stem: Which process is a physical change?

parts.prompt: Which process is a physical change?

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: cracking of an alkane to give an alkene

common_mistakes.feedback: Combustion of carbon (C + O2 -> CO2) forms a new substance, carbon dioxide. Any burning/combustion reaction is a chemical change, not a physical one.
Cracking breaks long hydrocarbon molecules into shorter, different molecules (an alkane + an alkene). The products are new substances, so cracking is a chemical change.
Rusting is iron reacting with oxygen and water to form iron oxide — a new substance. So rusting is a chemical change, not a physical one.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: C — fractional distillation of petroleum.
A physical change does not form a new substance; it only changes the state, form or situation of the substance. Fractional distillation of petroleum just heats the mixture so each hydrocarbon boils off at its own boiling point and is then condensed. The same hydrocarbon molecules come out — no new substance is made — so this is a physical change.

Why the others are chemical changes (a new substance is formed):
• A — combustion of carbon: C + O2 → CO2 (new substance, CO2).
• B — cracking: long alkane → shorter alkane + alkene (new, different molecules).
• D — rusting: iron + oxygen (+ water) → iron oxide (rust).

Combustion: a chemical reaction in which a substance reacts with oxygen and releases energy; combustion is exothermic.

questions.stem: A reaction pathway diagram for a reaction is shown (energy vs progress of reaction with marked energy change X from peak down to products). Which statement describes and explains energy change X?

parts.prompt: Which statement describes and explains energy change X?

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: Thermal energy is given out as bonds break.

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: Thermal energy is given out as bonds form.

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: Thermal energy is taken in as bonds break.

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: Thermal energy is taken in as bonds form.

common_mistakes.feedback: Bond breaking always takes energy IN (not out). It is the uphill part of the diagram, not the downhill arrow X. Try again.

You have the right idea that bond breaking takes energy IN, but that is the UPHILL part of the diagram (reactants to top of hump). Arrow X points DOWNWARDS from the peak to the products line, so it must be energy being given OUT, not taken in.
Bond forming gives energy OUT, not in. New bonds make the products more stable, so they sit lower on the energy axis. That is why X points downward.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: B (thermal energy is given out as bonds form)
On a reaction pathway diagram, the uphill climb from reactants to the top of the hump is bond breaking - this takes energy IN.
The downhill drop from the top of the hump to the products is bond forming - this gives energy OUT.
Arrow X points downward from the peak to the products line, so X shows thermal energy being given out as new bonds form in the products.
Rule to remember: breaking bonds takes energy in, forming bonds gives energy out.

Activation energy (Ea): the minimum energy that colliding particles must have for a reaction to occur.

questions.stem: Which description explains why a reaction involves reduction?

parts.prompt: Which description explains why a reaction involves reduction?

common_mistakes.feedback: A loss of mass can happen in lots of reactions (e.g. a gas escapes when a carbonate decomposes). 'Mass decreases' is not the definition of reduction. Reduction is about what happens to oxygen (or electrons).
A drop in pH means a solution is becoming more acidic - that's about acids, not about redox. Reduction is defined as loss of oxygen (or gain of electrons), not a change in pH.
A temperature drop means the reaction is endothermic - that's a topic about energy, not redox. Reduction means a substance has LOST oxygen (or gained electrons).

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: B - oxygen is lost.
In the chapter's oxygen view, reduction = loss of oxygen (and oxidation = gain of oxygen).
Remember the mnemonic OIL RIG: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain - of electrons.
So any description that says a substance has lost oxygen is describing reduction.
chapter_refs: C6.03 Redox reactions
Reduction (oxygen view): the loss of oxygen by an element or compound during a chemical reaction.
OIL RIG mnemonic: Oxidation Is Loss of electrons, Reduction Is Gain of electrons.

questions.stem: Hydrogen peroxide, H2O2, decomposes into water and oxygen when a catalyst is added.

parts.prompt: Balance the symbol equation for the reaction: ......H2O2 -> ......H2O + O2.

required_working_concepts.concept: Balanced equation with coefficients 2, 2, 1

common_mistakes.feedback: That is the unbalanced version. Count the atoms - you need a 2 in front of H2O2 and a 2 in front of H2O to balance both hydrogen and oxygen.
You balanced the left side but not the right. Putting 2 in front of H2O2 gives 4H and 4O on the left; you need 2H2O as well on the right so both sides have 4H and 4O.

parts.model_answer_html:
2H2O2 → 2H2O + O2
Check: left = 4 H + 4 O; right = 4 H + 4 O. Balanced.
chapter_refs: Decomposition of hydrogen peroxide (catalysed by MnO2): 2H2O2(l) -> 2H2O(l) + O2(g).
C6.02 Rate of reaction

parts.prompt: The oxygen is collected and tested. Describe the test for oxygen gas. State the observation for the positive result.
accepted_phrasings: place a glowing splint in the gas; it relights
bring a glowing splint to the mouth of the tube; it relights

required_working_concepts.concept: Test = glowing splint (not burning/lit splint)
Positive observation = splint relights

common_mistakes.feedback: For oxygen the test uses a GLOWING splint (just smoking, no flame), not a lit splint. Lit/burning splint is the test for hydrogen, where you hear a squeaky pop.
Squeaky pop is the test for hydrogen gas. For oxygen, a GLOWING splint RELIGHTS.
Make sure you say the splint must be GLOWING (not lit). The positive sign is that a glowing splint relights in oxygen.

parts.model_answer_html:
Test: place a glowing splint at the mouth of the tube.
Positive observation: the splint relights.

parts.prompt: Anhydrous cobalt(II) chloride is used to test for water. State the colour observed for a positive result.

required_working_concepts.concept: Positive colour = pink

common_mistakes.feedback: Blue is the starting colour of the ANHYDROUS (dry) cobalt(II) chloride. When water is added (positive result) it turns PINK.
White is the colour change for anhydrous COPPER(II) sulfate turning blue with water. For cobalt(II) chloride the colour on adding water is PINK.

parts.model_answer_html: The colour observed is pink (anhydrous blue → hydrated pink).
chapter_refs: Decomposition of hydrogen peroxide (catalysed by MnO2): 2H2O2(l) -> 2H2O(l) + O2(g).

parts.prompt: Describe what is meant by a catalyst.

required_working_concepts.concept: A catalyst increases the rate (speeds up) the reaction
A catalyst is chemically unchanged / not used up at the end of the reaction
A catalyst provides an alternative pathway with a lower activation energy (mechanism)

common_mistakes.feedback: You need to mention that a catalyst SPEEDS UP the reaction (or increases the RATE). Just saying 'it helps' is not enough for an IGCSE mark.
For the second mark, include that the catalyst is NOT USED UP / is chemically UNCHANGED at the end of the reaction. That is a key part of the definition.
A catalyst does not start a reaction - the reaction already happens slowly without it. A catalyst makes the reaction happen FASTER and is unchanged afterwards.

parts.model_answer_html: A catalyst is a substance that increases the rate of a chemical reaction (by providing an alternative pathway with a lower activation energy) and is chemically unchanged at the end of the reaction.
chapter_refs: Catalyst: a substance that increases the rate of a chemical reaction by providing an alternative pathway with a lower activation energy and is itself chemically unchanged at the end of the reaction.
Activation energy (Ea): the minimum energy that colliding particles must have for a reaction to occur.
C6.02 Rate of reaction

parts.prompt: The temperature of the hydrogen peroxide is increased. All other conditions stay the same. Predict the effect of this change on the reaction.
accepted_phrasings: oxygen is given off more quickly

required_working_concepts.concept: Rate increases / reaction speeds up

common_mistakes.feedback: Heating ALWAYS speeds up chemical reactions (assuming nothing else changes). Particles move faster and more collisions have energy >= Ea.
Temperature has a strong effect on reaction rate. Even a 10 degrees C rise can roughly double the rate, so saying 'no change' is not correct.

parts.model_answer_html:
The reaction goes faster - the rate of decomposition of H2O2 increases.
Why: at higher T the particles have more kinetic energy, collide more often, and a greater proportion of collisions have energy ≥ Ea.
chapter_refs: Higher temperature increases reaction rate because (a) particles move faster so collide more often and (b) a greater proportion of collisions exceed Ea (this second effect dominates).
Collision theory: the idea that a chemical reaction takes place only when reactant particles collide with each other with an energy at least equal to the activation energy.
C6.02 Rate of reaction

questions.stem: Magnesium carbonate reacts with dilute acid. (continuation of Q4) (b)(ii) State and explain how the size of the magnesium carbonate pieces affects the rate of this reaction. (c) Dot-and-cross diagram of ionic bonding in MgCl2 — outside chapter C6 scope.

parts.prompt: State and explain how the size of the magnesium carbonate pieces affects the rate of this reaction.

required_working_concepts.concept: State the direction of the effect: smaller pieces (or powder) give a FASTER rate; larger pieces give a slower rate.
Link piece size to surface area: smaller pieces have a LARGER TOTAL SURFACE AREA (for the same mass).
Explain using collision theory: a larger surface area means more frequent collisions (or more successful collisions per second) between acid particles and the solid, so the rate is faster.

common_mistakes.feedback: Be careful — the mass of MgCO3 is the SAME in both experiments; what changes is the surface area because the same mass is split into smaller pieces. Don't say 'more mass reacts' — say 'larger total surface area' instead.
The key phrase the IGCSE marker is looking for is 'surface area'. Smaller pieces have a LARGER total surface area, and that is what actually changes the collision rate.
To get the explanation mark you need to mention collisions. Bigger surface area → acid particles collide with the solid MORE FREQUENTLY, so more successful collisions per second, so faster rate.
Careful — the question is about the SIZE OF THE SOLID, not the concentration of the acid. Keep the explanation about surface area of the solid, not about acid concentration.

parts.model_answer_html:
Smaller pieces of magnesium carbonate make the reaction faster.

For the same mass of magnesium carbonate, smaller pieces (or a powder) have a larger total surface area exposed to the acid.

Using collision theory: acid particles can only react when they collide with the solid. A larger surface area means there are more places where the acid can hit the MgCO3, so there are more frequent collisions per second and more successful collisions each second, so the rate of reaction is faster.

questions.stem: The equations describe the reactions that happen when iron is extracted in the blast furnace: C + O2 -> CO2; C + CO2 -> 2CO; Fe2O3 + 3CO -> 2Fe + 3CO2. What is a correct description of one of these reactions?

parts.prompt: Which statement is a correct description of one of the three blast-furnace equations?

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: Carbon dioxide is oxidised to form carbon monoxide.

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: Iron(III) oxide is reduced by carbon dioxide.

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: Carbon reacts with carbon monoxide to form carbon dioxide.

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: Carbon monoxide removes oxygen from iron(III) oxide.

common_mistakes.feedback: Check the oxygen count. In C + CO2 -> 2CO, CO2 LOSES an oxygen atom to become CO, so CO2 is REDUCED, not oxidised. A describes the opposite change.
Read the third equation carefully: Fe2O3 + 3CO -> 2Fe + 3CO2. Fe2O3 is reduced by CO (carbon monoxide), not by CO2 (carbon dioxide). CO2 is actually a product of this reaction.
The second equation is C + CO2 -> 2CO: carbon reacts with carbon DIOXIDE to form carbon MONOXIDE. Option C swaps CO and CO2 around, so it describes the reverse of what happens.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: D - Carbon monoxide removes oxygen from iron(III) oxide.
The third equation is Fe2O3 + 3CO -> 2Fe + 3CO2.
- Fe2O3 has lost its oxygen (Fe2O3 -> Fe), so Fe2O3 is reduced.
- CO has gained an oxygen (CO -> CO2), so CO is oxidised.
So CO is the substance that removes oxygen from iron(III) oxide - option D.
Quick check of the others: A is wrong because CO2 is reduced to CO (loses oxygen); B is wrong because CO (not CO2) reduces Fe2O3; C is wrong because the reaction goes C + CO2 -> 2CO, not the reverse.
chapter_refs: C6.03 Redox reactions
Oxidation (oxygen view): the gain of oxygen by an element or compound during a chemical reaction.
Reduction (oxygen view): the loss of oxygen by an element or compound during a chemical reaction.
Reduction of zinc oxide by carbon: ZnO(s) + C(s) -> Zn(s) + CO(g). Zinc is reduced; carbon is oxidised.

questions.stem: Fig. 6.1 shows the combustion of magnesium ribbon in air held by tongs; a white powder of magnesium oxide is formed.

parts.prompt: Name the gas that reacts with the magnesium.

required_working_concepts.concept: Correct gas is oxygen (the reactive gas in air).

common_mistakes.feedback: Air is a mixture of gases, but the exam wants the specific gas that reacts. Write 'oxygen' (about 21% of air) — that is the gas that actually combines with magnesium to form magnesium oxide.
Nitrogen makes up most of the air, but it is very unreactive. The gas in air that reacts with burning magnesium to form magnesium oxide is oxygen.
Carbon dioxide is only a tiny part of air. Burning magnesium combines with oxygen to form magnesium oxide: 2Mg + O2 -> 2MgO. The correct answer is oxygen.

parts.model_answer_html:
Oxygen (O2).
The chemical equation is 2Mg(s) + O2(g) → 2MgO(s).

common_mistakes.feedback: Acidic oxides are formed by non-metals (for example CO2 or SO2). Magnesium is a metal, so its oxide is the opposite: a basic oxide that reacts with acids to give a salt plus water.
'Aqueous' just means 'dissolved in water' — it is not a type of oxide. Magnesium oxide is a metal oxide, which makes it basic.

basic.

parts.prompt: This reaction is exothermic. Describe what is meant by exothermic.
accepted_phrasings: heat is given out to the surroundings
energy is released to the surroundings

required_working_concepts.concept: Energy (heat) is given out / released to the surroundings.

common_mistakes.feedback: That is the definition of endothermic. Exothermic is the opposite: heat (energy) is given out to the surroundings, so the surroundings get hotter.
Absorbing heat would be endothermic. Exothermic means heat energy is released/given out to the surroundings.
For the mark, say where the heat goes. Write that heat energy is 'given out to the surroundings' — that shows you understand exothermic.

parts.model_answer_html: Exothermic means heat (energy) is given out to the surroundings, so the surroundings get hotter. Burning magnesium gives out a lot of heat and a bright white flame.

questions.stem: The reaction pathway diagrams for reaction X and for reaction Y are shown. Which statement about the reactions is correct? (Reaction X shows a pathway with reactants at higher energy than products; reaction Y shows a pathway with products higher than reactants.)

parts.prompt: Which statement about the reactions is correct?

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: Reaction X has a greater activation energy than reaction Y.

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: Reaction X is endothermic and reaction Y is exothermic.

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: The overall energy change in reaction X is much greater than in reaction Y.

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: The temperature increases during reaction X and decreases during reaction Y.

common_mistakes.feedback: Activation energy is the HEIGHT OF THE HUMP measured from the reactants line up to the peak. In these two diagrams the humps look about the same size, so we cannot say X has a greater activation energy. The key difference between X and Y is NOT the hump height but where the products end up - lower for X, higher for Y.

You have it the wrong way round. In reaction X the products sit BELOW the reactants, so energy is given out - X is exothermic. In reaction Y the products sit ABOVE the reactants, so energy is taken in - Y is endothermic. So it is X exothermic, Y endothermic.

The overall energy change is the vertical gap between the reactants line and the products line. In these diagrams the gap in X and the gap in Y look similar in size, so we cannot say one is 'much greater' than the other. The real difference is the DIRECTION of the change (down for X, up for Y), not the size.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: D (temperature increases during X, decreases during Y)
In reaction X, the products sit LOWER on the energy axis than the reactants. Energy is given out to the surroundings, so reaction X is exothermic and the surroundings (and the thermometer) get HOTTER.
In reaction Y, the products sit HIGHER than the reactants. Energy is taken in from the surroundings, so reaction Y is endothermic and the surroundings (and the thermometer) get COLDER.
Shortcut: products BELOW reactants = exothermic = temperature up. Products ABOVE reactants = endothermic = temperature down.

Activation energy (Ea): the minimum energy that colliding particles must have for a reaction to occur.
Chemical change (chemical reaction): a change in which one or more new substances are formed; usually difficult to reverse and accompanied by an energy change.

questions.stem: Solid lumps of zinc react with dilute hydrochloric acid. Which change decreases the rate of this reaction?

parts.prompt: Solid lumps of zinc react with dilute hydrochloric acid. Which change decreases the rate of this reaction?

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: adding a catalyst to the reaction mixture

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: increasing the concentration of the hydrochloric acid

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: increasing the size of the zinc lumps

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: increasing the temperature of the hydrochloric acid

common_mistakes.feedback: A catalyst SPEEDS UP reactions (lowers the activation energy). Adding one would INCREASE the rate, not decrease it.
Higher concentration of acid = more H+ particles per unit volume = more frequent collisions with zinc, so the rate INCREASES. This is not the answer.
Raising the temperature always speeds reactions up: particles move faster and more collisions have energy >= Ea. This is not the answer.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: C - increasing the SIZE of the zinc lumps gives a smaller surface area for the same mass. Fewer Zn atoms are exposed to the acid, so there are fewer collisions per second and the rate decreases.
A (catalyst) and D (higher T) both speed it up. B (higher concentration) also speeds it up.

parts.prompt: Calcium carbonate reacts with dilute hydrochloric acid to make calcium chloride, carbon dioxide and water. What is the balanced symbol equation and correct state symbols for this reaction?

common_mistakes.feedback: Option A is NOT balanced. CaCl2 contains two chlorines, so you need 2HCl on the left. Check: Cl atoms on left = 1, on right = 2 — unbalanced.

Option B uses HCl(l) — hydrochloric acid is an aqueous solution, so it should be HCl(aq), not (l). CaCl2 also dissolves in the acid so it is (aq), not (s). And water formed in the flask is a liquid, H2O(l), not (aq).
Option D is balanced but the state symbols are wrong. Dilute HCl is (aq) not (l); dissolved CaCl2 is (aq) not (s); the water formed is (l) not (aq).

Answer: C — CaCO3(s) + 2HCl(aq) → CaCl2(aq) + CO2(g) + H2O(l)

Balancing: CaCl2 needs 2 Cl atoms, so there must be 2HCl on the left. That also supplies the 2 H atoms needed to make one H2O.

questions.stem: Which two substances form when magnesium reacts with steam?

B is wrong. When magnesium reacts with steam, the water is reduced (it loses its oxygen), so hydrogen gas is released, not oxygen. The correct answer is C — magnesium oxide and hydrogen.

parts.model_answer_html:
C — magnesium oxide and hydrogen.

parts.prompt: Complete the sentences about the reaction of magnesium with steam. Use words from the list: anions, cations, covalent, electrons, ionic, neutrons, neutralisation, oxidation, protons, reduction. (1) Magnesium atoms lose ______ to form positive ions. (2) These positive ions are known as ______. (3) Positive ions and negative ions attract each other and form ______ bonds. (4) Magnesium atoms gain oxygen. This type of reaction is ______.

Blank 2 = cations (positive ions are cations)
Blank 3 = ionic (Mg2+ and O2- form an ionic bond by electrostatic attraction)
Blank 4 = oxidation (gain of oxygen = oxidation by the oxygen definition)

parts.model_answer_html:
(1) electrons — Mg atoms lose 2 electrons (OIL: Oxidation Is Loss) to become Mg2+.
(2) cations — positive ions are called cations.

(4) oxidation — gaining oxygen is the oxygen-view definition of oxidation.

questions.stem: Which statement explains why the rate of a reaction is greater at a higher temperature?

parts.prompt: Which statement explains why the rate of a reaction is greater at a higher temperature?

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: More colliding particles have the minimum energy to react.

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: The frequency of collisions between reacting particles decreases.

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: There are more reacting particles per unit volume.

common_mistakes.feedback: The activation energy is a property of the reaction - heating the mixture does NOT change Ea. What changes is the number of particles that have enough energy to overcome Ea.
The opposite is true: at higher T particles move faster and collide MORE often, not less. And fewer collisions would slow the reaction down, not speed it up.
Heating does not add more particles. More particles per unit volume is what happens when you increase CONCENTRATION or PRESSURE, not temperature.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: B - at higher temperature particles have more kinetic energy, so a greater proportion of collisions have energy ≥ the activation energy Ea. More of the collisions are successful, so the rate of reaction increases.
(Particles also collide a little more often, but the bigger effect is the larger fraction of collisions that exceed Ea.)
chapter_refs: C6.02 Rate of reaction
Collision theory: the idea that a chemical reaction takes place only when reactant particles collide with each other with an energy at least equal to the activation energy.
Activation energy (Ea): the minimum energy that colliding particles must have for a reaction to occur.
Higher temperature increases reaction rate because (a) particles move faster so collide more often and (b) a greater proportion of collisions exceed Ea (this second effect dominates).

questions.stem: The equation for the complete combustion of ethane is: 2C2H6 + 7O2 -> 4CO2 + 6H2O. Which statement about this reaction is correct? A) Ethane is oxidised because it gains oxygen. B) Ethane is reduced because carbon-hydrogen bonds are broken. C) It is endothermic because thermal energy is transferred to the surroundings. D) It is exothermic because thermal energy is taken in from the surroundings.

parts.prompt: Which statement about the combustion of ethane is correct?

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: Ethane is oxidised because it gains oxygen.

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: Ethane is reduced because carbon-hydrogen bonds are broken.

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: It is endothermic because thermal energy is transferred to the surroundings.

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: It is exothermic because thermal energy is taken in from the surroundings.

common_mistakes.feedback: Reduction is the LOSS of oxygen (or the gain of electrons), not just 'breaking bonds'. In this reaction ethane gains oxygen, so it is oxidised, not reduced.
Statement C contradicts itself. If thermal energy is transferred TO the surroundings, the reaction is EXOTHERMIC (not endothermic). Combustion always gives out heat.
Statement D also contradicts itself. Taking in thermal energy is ENDOTHERMIC. Exothermic means energy is given OUT. Combustion is exothermic because heat is released, not taken in.

parts.model_answer_html:
The answer is A.
In the equation 2C2H6 + 7O2 → 4CO2 + 6H2O, the carbon atoms in ethane end up bonded to oxygen (in CO2), so ethane has gained oxygen.
The chapter definition says oxidation = gain of oxygen, so ethane is oxidised.
The other options are wrong: reduction is loss of oxygen (not 'breaking C-H bonds'), and combustion is exothermic because heat is given OUT to the surroundings.

Oxidation (oxygen view): the gain of oxygen by an element or compound during a chemical reaction.
Reduction (oxygen view): the loss of oxygen by an element or compound during a chemical reaction.

questions.stem: Which type of reaction occurs when alkenes react with bromine?

parts.prompt: Which type of reaction occurs when alkenes react with bromine?

common_mistakes.feedback: Not combustion. Combustion means burning in oxygen (for example methane + O2 → CO2 + H2O). There is no oxygen in this reaction — only the alkene and bromine — so it cannot be combustion.

Not displacement. Displacement is when a more reactive element pushes a less reactive element out of its compound (like zinc displacing copper from CuSO4). Here, nothing is being kicked out — both bromine atoms end up inside the new product molecule.

Not polymerisation. Polymerisation would join many alkene molecules into one long chain (like lots of ethene forming poly(ethene)). In this reaction only one alkene molecule reacts with one Br2 molecule to make one small product, so it cannot be polymerisation.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: A — addition.
Alkenes contain a C=C double bond. When an alkene reacts with bromine (Br2), the two Br atoms add on across the double bond: the C=C becomes a C–C single bond and one Br atom bonds to each carbon, forming 1,2-dibromoalkane. No atoms are lost — everything that goes in ends up in the single new product.

You can rule the others out using the chapter definitions:
• Combustion needs oxygen — there is no O2 here.
• Displacement needs a more reactive element to push a less reactive one out of its compound — nothing is displaced here.
• Polymerisation joins many small molecules into a long chain — only one alkene + one Br2 react here, so no chain forms.

The orange-to-colourless colour change (bromine water decolourising) is the classic school test for an alkene — it works because of this addition reaction, and it counts as a chemical change because a new substance (1,2-dibromoalkane) is formed.
chapter_refs: C6.01 Physical and chemical changes
Chemical change (chemical reaction): a change in which one or more new substances are formed; usually difficult to reverse and accompanied by an energy change.
Combustion: a chemical reaction in which a substance reacts with oxygen and releases energy; combustion is exothermic.
Displacement reaction: a reaction in which a more reactive element displaces a less reactive element from a compound or solution.

Count the oxygen atoms. 2 x Fe2O3 has 6 oxygen atoms. Each O2 has 2, so you need 3 O2 molecules to give 6 oxygen atoms.
Oxygen is a gas at room temperature, not a liquid. The correct state symbol is (g).
Oxygen is a gas at room temperature, not a solid. The state symbol should be (g).

4Fe(s) + 3O2(g) -> 2Fe2O3(s)

So we need 4 Fe on the left, and 6 O atoms = 3 O2 molecules.

parts.prompt: Most cars burn fuels that contain carbon. Explain why using iron as a fuel may cause less harm to the environment than using fuels that contain carbon.

required_working_concepts.concept: Carbon-based fuels produce CO2 when burned, and CO2 is a greenhouse gas that contributes to global warming / climate change.
Burning iron produces only iron oxide (Fe2O3) as the product, so it does not release CO2 (or CO, or SO2) into the atmosphere.
The iron oxide produced can be converted back to iron (equation 2: Fe2O3 + 3H2 -> 2Fe + 3H2O), so iron can be recycled / reused as a fuel, unlike carbon fuels which are used up.

common_mistakes.feedback: A full answer should mention carbon dioxide (CO2). Burning carbon-based fuels produces CO2, which is a greenhouse gas.
To get the environmental point, link CO2 to the greenhouse effect / global warming / climate change. Just saying 'produces CO2' doesn't explain the harm.
For 3 marks, mention that the iron oxide product can be reduced back to iron (equation 2), so iron can be reused/recycled - carbon fuels can't.
Good idea, but be careful with the word 'renewable'. A better way is: iron is RECYCLABLE because equation 2 turns the iron oxide back into iron, so the same iron can be burned again.

parts.model_answer_html:
Three marking points, any three:
1. Burning fuels that contain carbon produces carbon dioxide (CO2), which is a greenhouse gas that adds to global warming / climate change. (Incomplete combustion can also make toxic CO, and sulfur impurities can make SO2 that causes acid rain.)
2. When iron burns (equation 1: 4Fe + 3O2 -> 2Fe2O3) the only product is solid iron(III) oxide. No CO2, CO or SO2 is released into the atmosphere.
3. The iron(III) oxide can be turned back into iron using equation 2 (Fe2O3 + 3H2 -> 2Fe + 3H2O), so iron can be recycled and used again as a fuel. Carbon fuels are used up once and release their carbon into the atmosphere.

parts.prompt: Define activation energy.

required_working_concepts.concept: Minimum energy
that colliding (reactant) particles must have for a reaction to happen

parts.prompt: Describe what is meant by a catalyst.

required_working_concepts.concept: Speeds up / increases the rate of the reaction (by lowering the activation energy / providing an alternative pathway)
Is itself chemically unchanged at the end of the reaction / not used up

common_mistakes.feedback: Saying only 'a catalyst lowers activation energy' scores about half. You also need to say WHAT EFFECT this has - it SPEEDS UP the reaction (increases the rate).
You are missing the second mark. A catalyst is not used up / is chemically UNCHANGED at the end of the reaction. Always pair 'speeds up reaction' with 'not used up'.
A catalyst does NOT start a reaction that otherwise wouldn't happen. It makes a reaction that is already possible go FASTER by giving the particles an easier route (lower activation energy).
A catalyst does not raise the temperature. It lowers the ACTIVATION ENERGY, so a bigger fraction of collisions at the SAME temperature are successful.

questions.stem: The reaction between aluminium and iron(III) oxide is exothermic. A flame is used to provide the activation energy to start the reaction. Fig. 5.1 shows the incomplete reaction pathway diagram for this reaction.

parts.prompt: Complete Fig. 5.1. Include: the energy level of the products; a labelled arrow to show the activation energy, Ea; a labelled arrow to show the overall energy change of the reaction.

required_working_concepts.concept: Products line drawn BELOW the reactants line (reaction is exothermic, so products are lower in energy).
Activation energy Ea arrow: a vertical arrow going from the REACTANTS energy level UP to the TOP of the hump (the energy barrier / transition state), labelled Ea.
hints: Ea from reactants to top of hump
Ea arrow to top of curve

required_working_concepts.concept: Overall energy change arrow: a vertical arrow going from the REACTANTS level DOWN to the PRODUCTS level, labelled 'overall energy change' (or delta H, energy released). Because the reaction is exothermic, it points downward.
hints: arrow from reactants down to products

common_mistakes.feedback: If the products line is ABOVE the reactants, the reaction would be ENDOTHERMIC (takes in energy). This reaction is described as EXOTHERMIC, so the products line must be LOWER than the reactants line.

The activation energy arrow must start from the REACTANTS line (not the products line) and go UP to the top of the hump. Ea is the extra energy the particles need before they can react, measured from where they start.
Don't forget the curve needs a HUMP (peak) between reactants and products. Without the hump there is no activation energy to show.
You also need to label the second arrow 'overall energy change' (or delta H). This arrow goes from the reactants level straight down to the products level and shows how much energy is released.

parts.model_answer_html:
You need three things on the diagram:

1. Products line (1 mark). Draw a short horizontal line LOWER than the reactants line, labelled "products". The reaction is exothermic, so products end up at a lower energy.

2. Activation energy arrow, Ea (1 mark). Draw a smooth curve from the reactants up over a hump and down to the products. Then draw a vertical arrow from the reactants line UP to the TOP of the hump, labelled Ea. This is the energy the flame has to supply to get the reaction going.

3. Overall energy change arrow (1 mark). Draw a second vertical arrow from the reactants line DOWN to the products line, labelled "overall energy change" (or ΔH). Because products are lower than reactants, the arrow points down and shows that energy is released.

Remember: the flame only has to provide Ea. Once started, the energy released (the overall change) is more than Ea, so the reaction keeps going by itself.

parts.prompt: Complete the word equation: ______ + ______ -> magnesium oxide + ______.

required_working_concepts.concept: Reactants named as magnesium and steam (or water).
Third product named as hydrogen.

common_mistakes.feedback: Oxygen is not a reactant here — the oxygen needed comes from the water itself. Steam (water) is the reactant that provides oxygen to the magnesium, and hydrogen gas is the product.
Magnesium only forms magnesium hydroxide when it reacts slowly with cold liquid water. With hot steam, the product is magnesium oxide and hydrogen gas.
You need the third product. When steam is reduced (loses its oxygen to the magnesium), the leftover is hydrogen gas. Word equation: magnesium + steam -> magnesium oxide + hydrogen.

parts.model_answer_html:
magnesium + steam (water) → magnesium oxide + hydrogen.
Symbol equation: Mg(s) + H2O(g) → MgO(s) + H2(g).

required_working_concepts.concept: Names apparatus with a volume scale that measures gas directly (gas syringe, inverted measuring cylinder, or burette over water).

common_mistakes.feedback: A balance measures mass, not gas volume. To measure the volume of hydrogen directly, use a piece of apparatus with a volume scale — a gas syringe is the simplest choice.
A beaker is not accurate and has no fine volume scale. Use a gas syringe (or an inverted measuring cylinder over water) to measure the gas volume directly.
The question says the gas jar does not let you measure volume directly. You need something with a volume scale — a gas syringe is the standard answer.

parts.prompt: Fill the four blanks from the word list (anions, cations, covalent, electrons, ionic, neutrons, neutralisation, oxidation, protons, reduction): magnesium atoms lose ____ to form positive ions; these positive ions are known as ____; positive and negative ions attract each other and form ____ bonds; magnesium atoms gain oxygen, this type of reaction is ____.

required_working_concepts.concept: Blank 1 is electrons (loss of electrons = oxidation).
Blank 2 is cations (positive ions).
Blank 3 is ionic (ionic bonds between + and - ions).
Blank 4 is oxidation (gain of oxygen).

common_mistakes.feedback: Atoms do not lose or gain protons in a chemical reaction — that would change the element itself. In redox, atoms lose or gain electrons. Use 'electrons' for blank 1.
Anions are negative ions. Magnesium loses electrons to form a positive ion (Mg2+), and positive ions are called cations.
Covalent bonds form when atoms share electrons (usually between non-metals). A bond between a metal ion (+) and a non-metal ion (-) is an ionic bond.
Reduction is the loss of oxygen (or gain of electrons). Magnesium gains oxygen to become MgO, so this reaction is oxidation, not reduction.

parts.model_answer_html:
1) electrons — OIL: Oxidation Is Loss of electrons.
2) cations — positive ions are called cations.
3) ionic — oppositely charged ions attract in ionic bonds.
4) oxidation — gain of oxygen is oxidation.

no hydrogen is collected; copper is below hydrogen in reactivity

common_mistakes.feedback: Copper does not react with steam to give copper oxide — in fact, hydrogen can reduce copper oxide back to copper (CuO + H2 -> Cu + H2O). That shows copper is less reactive than hydrogen, so no reaction is seen.
Copper is less reactive than magnesium and less reactive than hydrogen. That is why copper will not react with steam — there is no observable reaction.
The mark for explanation needs the idea of reactivity. Write that copper is 'less reactive' than magnesium (and than hydrogen), so it does not react with steam.

parts.model_answer_html:
Observation: no visible reaction — no bubbles of gas, no glow, no change in the copper, no gas collected in the gas jar.
Explanation: copper is less reactive than magnesium (and less reactive than hydrogen), so it does not take oxygen from water/steam. From the chapter: CuO + H2 → Cu + H2O shows hydrogen can reduce copper oxide, meaning copper is below hydrogen in reactivity.

The instruction says 'to the nearest 0.5 degrees C'. Write your answer as a whole number or with a .5, e.g. 22.0 or 22.5.

Initial temperature ≈ 22.0 °C (to the nearest 0.5 °C).

questions.stem: Which label represents the activation energy for an endothermic reaction? The reaction pathway diagram shows energy on the y-axis and progress of reaction on the x-axis. The reactants line is on the left at a lower level, the curve rises over a peak (hump), and the products line is on the right at a higher level than the reactants (endothermic). Four arrows A, B, C, D are labelled on the diagram.

parts.prompt: Which label represents the activation energy for an endothermic reaction?

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: A (arrow from the x-axis up to the reactants line)

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: B (arrow from the reactants line up to the top of the peak)

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: C (arrow from the x-axis all the way up to the top of the peak)

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: D (arrow from the reactants-level dashed line up to the products line)

common_mistakes.feedback: Arrow A only goes from the x-axis up to the reactants line. That shows the energy of the reactants above a zero reference, NOT the activation energy. Activation energy is always measured from the reactants line up to the top of the hump.

Arrow C spans from the x-axis all the way up to the top of the peak. That is the total energy of the peak above zero, not the activation energy. Activation energy is only the part of the hump that sits ABOVE the reactants line.

Arrow D goes from the reactants-level dashed line up to the products line. That is the overall energy change (delta H) for this endothermic reaction, not the activation energy. Activation energy is the height of the hump above the reactants line.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: B

The activation energy (Ea) is the minimum energy that colliding particles must have for a reaction to take place. On a reaction pathway diagram it is the height of the hump measured from the reactants line straight up to the top of the peak.

The graph is endothermic because the products line is higher than the reactants line (energy is taken in).

Check each arrow:
• A: x-axis up to reactants line → energy of the reactants, not Ea.
• B: reactants line up to the top of the peak → this IS the activation energy.
• C: x-axis up to the top of the peak → total energy of the peak above zero, not Ea.
• D: reactants level up to products line → overall energy change (ΔH), not Ea.

questions.stem: Calcium oxide, CaO, is used to make cement. Calcium oxide is formed by the decomposition of calcium carbonate, CaCO3, at a very high temperature. The equation is CaCO3(s) -> CaO(s) + CO2(g).

parts.prompt: Suggest why making cement contributes to global warming.

required_working_concepts.concept: Carbon dioxide (CO2) is produced - either directly from the decomposition CaCO3 -> CaO + CO2, or from burning fuel to heat the kiln to the very high temperature required
CO2 is a greenhouse gas / causes the greenhouse effect / traps heat in the atmosphere, which causes global warming

common_mistakes.feedback: You need to name the specific gas: CARBON DIOXIDE (CO2). Saying just 'a gas is released' is too vague for a 'suggest' question about global warming.
You identified CO2, but you haven't said WHY CO2 matters. You must state that CO2 is a GREENHOUSE GAS / causes the greenhouse effect, which is what links it to global warming.
Mixing up two different issues. Ozone layer depletion (from CFCs) is separate from global warming (from CO2 and other greenhouse gases). CaCO3 decomposition does not produce CFCs.
The problem is not that oxygen is 'used up'. The problem is that CO2 is RELEASED. Focus on the new gas produced and the greenhouse effect.

parts.model_answer_html: Making cement releases carbon dioxide (CO2) in two ways: (1) the decomposition reaction itself produces CO2: CaCO3(s) → CaO(s) + CO2(g); and (2) fuels are burnt (combustion) to reach the very high temperature, which also releases CO2. CO2 is a greenhouse gas – it traps heat in the atmosphere and so contributes to global warming.
chapter_refs: C6.01 Physical and chemical changes
Combustion of methane: CH4(g) + 2O2(g) -> CO2(g) + 2H2O(g) (exothermic, redox).
Chemical change (chemical reaction): a change in which one or more new substances are formed; usually difficult to reverse and accompanied by an energy change.

parts.prompt: The reaction is endothermic. Explain what is meant by an endothermic reaction. Use ideas about making bonds and breaking bonds in your answer.

required_working_concepts.concept: Endothermic means energy (heat) is taken in/absorbed from the surroundings
Breaking bonds requires/takes in energy AND making bonds releases/gives out energy
Energy to break bonds in the reactants is GREATER than energy released when bonds form in the products
hints: energy to break bonds is greater
more energy to break than to make
breaking needs more energy than making

common_mistakes.feedback: That describes an EXOTHERMIC reaction. ENDOthermic means energy goes INTO the reaction (taken in from the surroundings), so the surroundings actually get cooler, not warmer.
The question specifically asks you to use ideas about breaking AND making bonds. You must mention BOTH: bond-breaking takes in energy, bond-making releases energy.

The final comparison is the key mark: for an ENDOthermic reaction the energy to BREAK the reactant bonds is GREATER than the energy released when the product bonds are formed. That's why overall energy is taken in.
You've swapped the two: breaking bonds REQUIRES (takes in) energy, making bonds RELEASES (gives out) energy. Think of breaking a strong chemical bond like pulling magnets apart - it costs energy.

parts.model_answer_html:
An endothermic reaction is one in which energy (heat) is taken in from the surroundings (so the surroundings cool down).
• Breaking bonds in the reactants takes in energy (energy has to be supplied to pull the atoms apart).
• Making bonds in the products releases energy (energy is given out when new bonds form).
For this reaction the energy needed to break the bonds in the reactants is greater than the energy released when the new bonds in the products are made, so overall energy is absorbed from the surroundings → endothermic.

questions.stem: The reaction between aluminium and iron(III) oxide is used to join iron railway lines together. 2Al(s) + Fe2O3(s) -> 2Fe(l) + Al2O3(s). Molten iron is produced in the reaction. Molten iron becomes solid when it cools.

parts.prompt: State how the equation shows that molten iron is produced.
accepted_phrasings: the state symbol after Fe is (l) which means liquid
Fe is shown as (l) meaning liquid/molten
iron has the state symbol (l) so it is a liquid

required_working_concepts.concept: Identify the state symbol (l) next to Fe as meaning liquid / molten

common_mistakes.feedback: Careful - (s) means SOLID. The state symbol telling you iron is molten is (l), which stands for LIQUID.
For the mark you need to say what the state symbol means. (l) stands for LIQUID, which is the same as molten.
True that thermite reactions get very hot, but the question asks what in the EQUATION shows iron is molten. The answer is the state symbol (l) after Fe.

parts.model_answer_html: The state symbol after Fe is (l), which means liquid (i.e. molten). So the equation itself tells you iron comes out as a liquid.
chapter_refs: Physical change: a change in the physical state, form or appearance of a substance that does not produce any new chemical substance.

parts.prompt: Use the equation to explain why this reaction is a redox reaction.
accepted_phrasings: Aluminium gains oxygen so Al is oxidised; iron(III) oxide loses oxygen so Fe2O3 is reduced
Al is oxidised (gains oxygen to form Al2O3); Fe2O3 is reduced (loses oxygen to form Fe)
oxidation and reduction happen together: Al oxidised, Fe2O3 reduced

required_working_concepts.concept: Aluminium is OXIDISED - it gains oxygen (goes from Al to Al2O3)
Iron(III) oxide is REDUCED - it loses oxygen (Fe2O3 goes to Fe)
hints: loss of oxygen by iron oxide

common_mistakes.feedback: You need the word OXIDISED somewhere. Say that aluminium is oxidised because it GAINS oxygen (Al becomes Al2O3).
You also need to say Fe2O3 is REDUCED because it LOSES oxygen (Fe2O3 becomes Fe). Redox needs BOTH: one thing oxidised AND another thing reduced.
Combustion is burning in oxygen gas. Here there is no O2 gas on the left of the equation; oxygen is being transferred from Fe2O3 to Al. The correct framing is oxidation + reduction = redox.
Swapped! Al GAINS oxygen (it is on its own on the left and ends up in Al2O3 on the right), so Al is OXIDISED, not reduced. Fe2O3 loses oxygen and is reduced.

parts.model_answer_html:
It is a redox reaction because oxidation and reduction happen at the same time:
• Aluminium is oxidised — Al gains oxygen (Al → Al2O3).
• Iron(III) oxide is reduced — Fe2O3 loses oxygen (Fe2O3 → Fe).
Oxidation and reduction together = redox.
chapter_refs: Oxidation (oxygen view): the gain of oxygen by an element or compound during a chemical reaction.
Reduction (oxygen view): the loss of oxygen by an element or compound during a chemical reaction.
Redox reaction: a reaction involving simultaneous reduction and oxidation.

parts.prompt: Complete the ionic equation for this reaction. Al + Fe^3+ -> ______ + ______

required_working_concepts.concept: Al3+ on the product side (aluminium loses 3 electrons -> Al3+)
Fe on the product side (Fe3+ gains 3 electrons -> Fe atom)

common_mistakes.feedback: Aluminium forms Al3+ ions, not Al+. Aluminium is in Group 13 and loses 3 electrons from its outer shell. Check OIL: oxidation is loss of 3 electrons here.
Fe3+ was on the LEFT (the reactant). On the right, Fe3+ has GAINED 3 electrons to become a neutral Fe atom (reduction). So the product is Fe, not Fe3+.
The ion given in this reaction is Fe3+ (iron(III)), not Fe2+. It gains 3 electrons to become Fe, not 2.
Make sure the aluminium product is written as Al3+ (with the 3+ charge). Without the charge the equation is not balanced for electrical charge.

parts.model_answer_html:
Al + Fe3+ → Al3+ + Fe
• Aluminium atom loses 3 electrons: Al → Al3+ + 3e− (oxidation).
• Iron(III) ion gains 3 electrons: Fe3+ + 3e− → Fe (reduction).
3 electrons leave Al and 3 electrons arrive at Fe3+, so electrons balance 1:1.
chapter_refs: OIL RIG mnemonic: Oxidation Is Loss of electrons, Reduction Is Gain of electrons.

parts.prompt: Explain the meaning of the symbol (III) in iron(III) oxide.
accepted_phrasings: the oxidation number of iron is +3
iron has an oxidation state of 3
iron is Fe3+ / charge on the iron ion is 3+
Roman numeral showing oxidation number of iron is 3

required_working_concepts.concept: (III) is a Roman numeral showing the oxidation number/state/charge on iron is +3 (i.e. iron is Fe3+)

common_mistakes.feedback: The (III) does NOT mean 3 iron atoms. Number of atoms is shown by subscripts like Fe2. Roman numerals in brackets show the oxidation number (charge) on the metal. Here (III) means iron has a +3 charge (Fe3+).
The (III) refers to IRON, not oxygen. It tells you the oxidation number of iron is +3, i.e. iron is Fe3+ in this compound.
You need to say the oxidation number / charge is THREE (+3). That is what the Roman numeral III means.

parts.model_answer_html: The (III) is a Roman numeral for 3. In chemistry it is written after a transition metal's name to show the oxidation number (charge) of the metal in that compound. So iron(III) oxide means iron has an oxidation number of +3, i.e. the iron is present as Fe3+ ions.

questions.stem: Which metal reacts most quickly with cold water?

parts.prompt: Which metal reacts most quickly with cold water?

common_mistakes.feedback: Sodium DOES react quickly with cold water, but potassium is EVEN more reactive. Potassium is below sodium in Group 1, so its reactivity is higher - the reaction is fast enough that the hydrogen gas produced ignites with a lilac flame.

Calcium reacts with cold water, but only at a moderate speed (small bubbles of hydrogen). Group 1 alkali metals (sodium, potassium) react much more quickly with cold water than calcium. The correct answer is potassium.
Magnesium reacts VERY SLOWLY with cold water - you would barely see any bubbles. Magnesium only reacts quickly with steam, not cold water. Potassium is by far the fastest of the four.

parts.model_answer_html:
C — potassium.
The reactivity order of these metals with cold water (fastest → slowest) is: potassium > sodium > calcium > magnesium.
Potassium is the Group 1 alkali metal lowest in the table of the four options, so it is the most reactive and reacts most quickly with cold water – so fast that the heat of reaction ignites the hydrogen produced (lilac flame).

rate of reaction = change in amount of reactant or product / change in time

questions.stem: Which reaction pathway diagram shows the activation energy for an exothermic reaction? Four reaction pathway diagrams A, B, C, D are given. In A, reactants are lower and products are higher (endothermic) and the 'activation energy' arrow points from the products dashed line up to the peak. In B, reactants are lower and products are higher (endothermic) and the 'activation energy' arrow spans from the reactants line up to the peak. In C, reactants are higher and products are lower (exothermic) and the 'activation energy' arrow goes from the reactants dashed line up to the peak. In D, reactants are higher and products are lower (exothermic) and the 'activation energy' arrow spans from the products line up to the peak.

parts.prompt: Which reaction pathway diagram shows the activation energy for an exothermic reaction?

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: A (endothermic profile; 'activation energy' shown as a small arrow from the products dashed line up to the peak)

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: B (endothermic profile; 'activation energy' shown as a large arrow from the reactants line up to the peak)

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: C (exothermic profile; 'activation energy' shown as an arrow from the reactants line up to the peak)

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: D (exothermic profile; 'activation energy' shown as a large arrow from the products line up to the peak)

common_mistakes.feedback: Diagram A is endothermic (products are higher than reactants), so it cannot be an exothermic reaction. An exothermic reaction must show the products LOWER than the reactants because energy is given out.

Diagram B does label the activation energy correctly (reactants line up to peak), but the profile is endothermic (products higher than reactants). The question asks for an exothermic reaction, so this is the wrong profile.

Diagram D is exothermic, but the 'activation energy' arrow is drawn from the PRODUCTS line up to the peak. That is the activation energy for the REVERSE reaction, not the forward reaction. Forward activation energy is always measured from the reactants line up to the peak.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: C

For this question you need TWO things at once:
• the profile must be exothermic (products lower than reactants because energy is given out), and
• the activation energy (Ea) must be drawn correctly, from the reactants line up to the top of the peak (the height of the hump above the reactants).

Check each diagram:
• A: products are above reactants → endothermic. Wrong profile.
• B: products are above reactants → endothermic. Wrong profile.
• C: products below reactants (exothermic) AND the 'activation energy' arrow runs from the reactants line up to the peak. Correct on both counts.
• D: products below reactants (exothermic), but the arrow starts at the products line, not the reactants line. That is the reverse Ea, not the forward Ea.

So the correct diagram is C.

questions.stem: Which arrow on the reaction pathway diagram represents the overall energy change for the reaction? The reaction pathway diagram shows energy on the y-axis and progress of reaction on the x-axis. The reactants line is on the left at a lower energy, the curve rises over a peak (hump), and the products line is on the right at a higher energy than the reactants (endothermic). Four arrows A, B, C, D are labelled on the diagram.

parts.prompt: Which arrow on the reaction pathway diagram represents the overall energy change for the reaction?

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: A (arrow from the x-axis up to the reactants line)

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: B (arrow from the reactants line up to the top of the peak)

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: C (arrow from the x-axis all the way up to the top of the peak)

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: D (arrow from the reactants-level dashed line up to the products line)

common_mistakes.feedback: Arrow A only reaches the reactants line from the x-axis. That is the energy of the reactants above a zero reference, not the overall energy change. The overall energy change is the gap between reactants and products lines.

Arrow B is the height of the hump measured from the reactants line up to the peak. That is the activation energy (Ea), not the overall energy change. The overall energy change compares reactants and products, not reactants and the peak.

Arrow C spans from the x-axis all the way up to the top of the peak. That is the total energy of the peak above zero, not the overall energy change. You want the gap between the reactants line and the products line.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: D

The overall energy change (also called ΔH) on a reaction pathway diagram is the vertical distance between the reactants line and the products line. For an endothermic reaction (products higher than reactants, like this one) it is measured upwards from the reactants line to the products line.

Check each arrow:
• A: x-axis up to reactants line → energy of the reactants, not the overall change.
• B: reactants line up to the peak → this is the activation energy (Ea), not the overall change.
• C: x-axis up to the peak → total energy of the peak above zero, not the overall change.
• D: reactants-level dashed line up to the products line → this IS the overall energy change.
